sentences.text: Yes.
The American side was much the larger, much the largest.
We had some very sharp people and some people that weren't too sharp.
The great regret of the court was that Justice Jackson attempted to do the main prosecutorial job against Goering, and he wasn't up to it.
Justice Jackson was William H.
Jackson, I think his name was.
He had been a judge on the Supreme Court--
Say it again.
William H.
Jackson.
I think it was William H.
And Harry Truman was looking for somebody to head up the US effort there.
And I've forgotten whether he had volunteered or Truman tapped him.
But anyway, with Truman's blessing, he organized the whole thing, and did, on the whole, a superb job.
But the point that I was working up to is this.
His training in the law was apparently in the scholarly side, not in the courtroom cross examination bit, you see.
And when he-- and he apparently attempted to cross-examine Goering in-- in the court.
Goering was the second man in the German Nazi hierarchy.
Hermann Wilhelm Goering had been a World War I German ace along with the Red Baron von Richthofen.
And so, you know, he was-- he was a nobleman, a German noble.
And but a very smart guy.
He was the second man after Hitler.
Yes.
And he was--
He was a huge man.
He came in a-- a drug addict.
And Colonel Andrus, who in charge-- who was in charge of the prison, cured him during-- during the trial, for which he received no thanks, of course.
But anyway, Goering was-- was a lecher, a criminal, but an extremely magnetic personality.
He had what is called charisma to an extraordinary degree.
And I-- I would-- a lot of people would come visit me and take a day to look at the trials.
And over and over again, I got this reaction.
If I just had a chance to get that guy up to the house for a beer some night, I'd love to hear him talk.
But of course, it was out of the question.
But anyway-- and then he was a lecher.
There was one Russian-- excuse me, French court reporter who had a very generous bustal development.
And she would wander into court, [LAUGHS] things waving, and Goering-- nnnnn.
It was the side of the courtroom.
They changed every half hour, these court reporters, you know.
And to see her come and go and Goering-- nnnnn.
He was just devouring her with his eyes.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Goering was, as I say, a smart man, and he very quickly realized that he was dealing with someone who didn't know the tricks of the trade of cross examination.
And as a result, he led Jackson down blind alleys and made him look ridiculous.
And it was really a rather shameful, sad procedure.
Jackson himself realized this, but only after the end of a long day of Goering's demonstration that he was superior to Jackson.
He had Jackson on the run.
And so Jackson called in Sir David Maxwell Fife.
The British prosecutor.
But whose life had been in the British courts and who knew exactly how to handle a recalcitrant witness.
And within an hour of the next morning, Sir David had Goering whining and cringing, which is what Jackson just couldn't quite do.
Oh, there were quite a number.
Drex Sprecher was one, although he's one of the minor ones.
Dodd's-- Christopher Dodd's father was one.
There was a colonel-- oh, there were about seven or eight of them.
What they did, they divided up the various different parts of the prosecution among these leading lawyers.
And each one of them then got a couple of assistants, and they would work over the documents, and they'd prepare a part of the case, you see.
Yes.
Sir David, and then there was Sir Hartley Shawcross.
He was quite good also.
But Sir David was the-- was the real expert.
Then there was-- I don't recall the Frenchman.
And the Russians were non-entities.
Yes.
The [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] US side had great difficulty getting reputable German lawyers to defend them.
They said, we don't-- we weren't Nazis, and we don't want anything to do with-- we're going to be associated with it.
Kansbeure was one I knew something about.
He had been a very famous, I think, Munich lawyer.
And during the war, he had gone into the German Navy.

And when he appeared in the Nuremberg courtroom, he was in a German Navy uniform, because the German Navy was kept in existence to clear the mines, you see, for something like a year after the end of the war.
And he-- anyway.
But Kansbeure was quite good.
And all of them were-- were quite able and quite personable.
I-- they were-- they were decent people.
And I think practically none of them were Nazis.
No.
Now, wait a minute.
As a matter of fact, Speer, one of them, Speer--
No, I'm talking about the defendant Speer.
Was one of our assistants.
We called him our assistant.
Speer was Hitler's second in command.
He had-- he had charge of all of these slave labor types.
He built things.
He was Hitler's architect.
He laid out that famous Nuremberg area where they had those [NON-ENGLISH] outside of Nuremberg.
You know, he designed that [INAUDIBLE].
He was a defendant.
Well--
That's-- that's-- Peter Uberall gave him that name, because he was fascinated by this system that we set up.
The-- the-- the-- the interpreting system.
And everybody said it wouldn't work.
And all of a sudden, Speer says, God, you know, it might work, you see.
And so he would listen very carefully.
And when we would put in a new interpreter, as we were doing every so often, he would-- we could see him tuning in and-- and-- and listen, listen, listen.
And then he would look over to me or to Peter, whoever was sitting there as-- as monitor, and say-- or and he was right every time.
He was saying, this one will do it, but that one won't, see?
And so we called him-- Peter called him, he's our assistant.
Well now, you've discussed Goering.
What kind of personality was Speer?
How did he strike you?
A very able man.
One of these kinds that will do practically anything if it forwards his personal ambition and his personal career.
I think if he had been a US citizen, he would have been on top of GE or GM or something like that.
He was really an extremely gifted administrator and executive.
Well, yeah.
I told you about the general.
What was his name?
Well, anyway.
Yes.
Yes.
The one that-- that sent that letter over to me about having his last statement translated.
Well, at the end of the trial, each defendant was given an opportunity to make a final statement.
They-- they were told not to take more than half an hour or so.

And before-- when this was announced, this general-- give me his name-- anyway-- looked over at me-- I was sitting at the end of the interpreter's bench-- and with a little piece of paper, sort of waving a piece of paper, which I took to mean he wanted to write a-- wanted me to take a note.
I-- I accepted.
I said yes, send the note over.

And in this note, he wrote down, he said, I have noticed that when your interpreters have a pre-prepared translation to read from, the effect is much better than if they just do it off the-- off the microphone.
If I prepare my final statement in advance, will you translate it for me.
So I wrote him back, of course, you give me the translation.
So he sent me this translation.
And I was looking for that a minute ago, and I-- I didn't find it.

What it was was a 3/4 page statement in which he said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I entered my military career, I was taught that the highest loyalty to which one owed-- the highest loyalty which one owed was one's own country.
Now, he says, I'm sitting here listening to this, and I hear people saying the highest entity to which one owes loyalty is humanity at large.
Now, he says, if the world is to proceed in this way and to be improved in this way, and he says, I belong then to the older generation which is left behind, and the new generation takes over.
He says, I will-- if that is to be the case, he says, I will gladly accept my punishment, whatever it may be, because I believe this is a progress to a higher level of moral standards.
Well, that's-- that's-- that's a man.
That's a human being.
And--
He was hung.
They had him dead to rights.
Hitler had-- had ordered that all of the Canadian [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] raiders-- what do they call them, that raided the coast of Dieppe, that were captured were to be executed.
In spite of the fact-- after they were captured.
And he-- I can't think of his name-- transmitted that order over his own signature.
Yeah, this general.
Which in itself is documentary prima facie evidence of his co-guilt.
And he was hung.
Oh, yes.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Streicher was the-- remember-- was the one that edited and published this Jew-baiting journal called [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [GERMAN].
The-- the Stormtrooper.
The Storm Attack, or the Attack Stormer, Stormtrooper, all right.
And he-- he put the most hideous things in there.
Lies, all of them.

Every-- you could look at a new edition of this, and there would be a big thing, and then always down in the right-hand corner, a Jew would back a innocent, virginal, white, German girl into a corner and rape her.
[INAUDIBLE] put your hand right there.
There it is, that story, with a new, different name and a different-- the Germans wouldn't have anything to do with him.
I mean, he was just another one of these animals.
He was-- he was executed.
Speer was given a-- I think it was a 15-year life-- a 15-year sentence.
Served it and was later released.
Executed.

He was-- he was condemned to be hanged, but he-- he sweet-talked, apparently-- well, nobody's quite sure-- a Texas captain, who was one of the guards, into telling him or indicating to him, to Goering, in advance, when the execution was to take place.
And the night before, he, Goering, took poison.
Killed himself.
They're arguing about it.

Apparently, they-- they claim that old Colonel Andrus, who was in charge of the prison, who wasn't too sharp, had all these people brought in when they first-- he would have them stripped and searched, including the probing of their bodily cavities, eh?
And somehow, the Nazis had prepared little vials of sodium cyanide-- potassium cyanide.
Poison, see?
And you would put it in your mouth and break the capsule, the capsule, and in about three minutes you'd be dead.
All right?
And so Goering apparently secreted this capsule in his belly button.
And they-- they-- they searched him-- and nobody knows for certain, but this is what they think happened.
They searched him, and he somehow palmed it.
And after they had searched the belly button, he put it back.
That's what they think happened.
Nobody knows for sure.
No.
No.
We had-- We had no particular difficulty about that.
From 9:00 to 5:00.
No, excuse me.
I guess it was 4:30.
I'm not sure.
Yes.
But I had, of course, an enormous amount of-- of administrative work to do.
And so my-- usually my visits to court would be fairly short.
I would duck in, and Peter would be there in charge.
Is everything going all right.
Peter Uberall, yeah.
Or Van Sastro, or Roth, or somebody.
And yeah, no problems.
And so I would go.
And if-- at another point, we had-- we had a full backup team of 12 interpreters at any one point in the-- in the court.
You see, every interpreter did one direction only.
For instance, if-- I know German and English.
I-- since English is my mother tongue, I would interpret only into English, from German into English, never from English into German.
That would be done by a native German, you see.
So that for every language, there would be two-- anyway-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we found we had to substitute, at times.
And the people that were substituted had to be aware of what was going on before they were brought in.
They just couldn't be brought in cold from outside.

So we had a special room set up outside, or adjacent to the courtroom, with wires into it so that they-- a full team of 12 would be sitting there, listening to what was going on, you see, with their-- with their headphones.
And the girls would be knitting, and the guys would be playing chess or something.
But-- but they-- they could tell, the interpreters could tell, well, so and so is about to get in trouble, and it sounds like I'm going to be called in, you see.
And so they would be all primed and set.
And if they did get in trouble, see, they'd be ready to step right in with minimum-- minimum delay.
Oh, no, no.
French, German, and Russian.
And English.
Yes.
But that didn't work at all well.
The Russians didn't have-- didn't want any non-Soviet to boss any Soviet citizen.
And so that led to the most incredible difficulties.
They wouldn't do what we wanted to.
They wouldn't-- well, we-- we-- we finally made up our minds that we could barely stand the men, but we liked the Russian girls.
They were delightful.
And every time there would be a-- a break in the court action or something like this, these-- these Russian girls would get together, and down [INAUDIBLE], they'd start singing Russian folk songs.
And you know-- [LAUGHS] and everybody loved them.
They're just delightful people.
We finally decided among ourselves that the German-- excuse me, the Russian men, to make it in the Soviet system, had to do so by boot-licking.
Now, you know a boot-licker is not exactly a attractive type of personality.
But the girls weren't under that imperative, and they could be natural.
They could be normal.
And they were delightful.
The French were very, very good.
Some of them were-- were cold as fish.
Oh, boy.
But they were superb, technically, as language people.
Well, he-- he was one of these very ambitious guys.
And he got the word that all sorts of jobs were opening up in the United States, and it was good to get back.
And so he left about-- just almost exactly halfway through.
I think it was in either February or March of 1946.
The trials began on the first of November, '45.
Wait, November?
It was.
Yeah, I think so.
And ended on the first of November, 1946.
And he understood-- he got the word that they were setting up similar things at United Nations, and he wanted to be in on the ground floor.
So he-- he left, and I took over.
Well, I had been his assistant.
And then I became head of the language division of the International Military Tribunal.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
Did I tell you about my promotion?
[LAUGHS] I--
Yeah, all right.
So I-- I was working with British, French, and Russian officers who were colonels and generals.
And here I was, a mere lieutenant commander, you see.
No, no, with them.
On the same level, you see.
And so-- and in the prosecution, I don't think-- the administration were all at least brigadier generals, you see.
[INAUDIBLE] They had a few colonels, maybe.
But anyway--
Yes.
Well, and also in the administrative side, outside the courtroom.
And so-- and so the Navy had this routine called spot promotion.

If a-- If a-- one-- a Navy officer got into a certain position, and they felt that a temporary promotion to the next higher rank was in order, they would give him what they called a, quote, 'spot' promotion for that spot.
And I got to thinking about it.
I thought, well, I ought to have a spot promotion.
So I sent away, and I got the Navy's pamphlet detailing how you would be promoted on a spot promotion.
And I looked through it, and I thought, well, I think I qualify.
So I went down to see General Jill.
I call him Jill.
His name was Gill, but you know, a jill is a half a pint, you see.
Well, that's about where we ranked the general--
He was an Army brigadier general.
Our army.
But he was my nearest superior, as far as we could figure out.
So anyway, I went down to see General Jill.
And I said General, I got the feeling that I ought to have-- ought to be qualified for a spot promotion.
And if you-- and I told him why.
And if you agree, now here's-- here's the Navy's regulations about how you do it.
And you write a letter, and you say this, and you say this, and you say this.
And here's the letter-- I mean, here's the Navy's regulations, and here are my-- my backup statistics on this and so forth.
And he says, oh, hell, Al, he says, you write the letter, I'll sign it.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah, I did.
And it worked.
So I became a commander in April of '46, I think.
Yeah.
But then-- then to my astonishment and pleasure, in-- in August of '46, I got a confirmation from the Navy saying that was a temporary promotion to commander.
Your temporary promotion has now been made permanent because you have served satisfactorily within this new rank.
Well, see, I was running my own career.
But no longer.
You can't do that anymore in the Navy.
I have a-- a diagram over there--
Yeah, well, you walk in, and the body of the court-- let's say you walk in the main door.
The body of the court was on the level that you walked in.
To your right, as you walked in, there were seats like in a movie theater, sort of going up in the back.
These were the spectators.
Then to the left, there would be a row of tables for the prosecutors, a separate table for each prosecutor.
The table might seat anywhere from six to-- or from two to three to six or eight lawyers, depending on what was going on.
And then on the far side of the room, elevated, were the judges, eight of them.
Four for each country.
That was on the far side.
And then to the-- to the left, as you walked in, so around to your left, would be the defendants in the dock.
And there were 21 of them, I think it was, or whatever it was.
And then behind the defendants, a little sort of a shelf where our 12 interpreters--
Oh, yes.
They had-- we had special guards.
They wore white helmets and white web belts and white pistol holsters and night sticks.
They all had night sticks.
Oh, yes.
Jammed all the time.
All the time.
A special part of the presstater-- the spectators' gallery was reserved for the press.
And there was another special little gallery up there reserved for photographers.
There were photographers there.
This was an old German [GERMAN], palace of justice, built by the Krauts in the 1870s.
And I am telling you, it was built of stone, block on block.
Now for instance, when we set up the wiring for the various different seats in the courtroom, you would normally put those under the floor and run them up [INAUDIBLE].
Uh-uh.
Not in that place, because it was solid stone.
So we had to lie up-- lay the wires on top of the stone.
And when a woman's high heel would hit one of those wires, you see, they could mash the insulation.
And we'd get what they call crosstalk.
You'd tune in what you hoped was English, and you'd get English, but with a strong Russian background.
Did that happen?
All the time.
And so we would constantly have to stop the court and repair the crosstalk here, over there-- and this was old, you see.
It had been used and stored in the tropics in Rio de Janeiro, and it was really secondhand stuff.
Yes.
In the courthouse, right opposite the-- the prison.
The-- the courthouse was a separate building from the prison.
There was a couple of alleyways, covered alleyways connected the two.
But if we'd look out my window, we'd have a real nice view of prisons.
No.
Yes, but only for a very short time.
I think I pointed out to you that you had to have this ironclad nervous control and assurance that under no circumstance were we ever gonna be in doubt about a word.
Now you've heard me two, three times grope for a name.
Well, you can't do that.
You have to have it boom, boom, boom, boom, instantly.
And I became very aware very quickly that I did not have this iron nervous assurance, ironclad nervous assurance that I would never do that, you see.
And so I-- I did it once or twice just to be able to say I had done it, but no, I didn't.
I purposely did not put myself in it.
Oh, Jesus.
That was a problem.
We had these-- we had, first of all, Army jeeps.
And then we-- we took over from the German army certain vehicles.
And from-- from the German army, I got a-- an Opel sedan, about 1937 or '38 model.
And I tell you, it was in terrible condition.
I claimed it had gone to-- to-- to Stalingrad and back.
It was in terrible shape.
And every time you'd run it up a hill, it'd catch fire.
And you'd sit down, and there was a spring that would hit you in the bottom of the you-know-what.
And oh, and so when Wallie Cohn came into the picture-- I told you about Wallie, didn't I?
Not yet.
No?
Well anyway, he appeared.

And when-- when Truman had sent out this message saying all US government agencies will cooperate, a lot of people used this as an excuse to get rid of their no-goods and has-beens and want-to-get-rid-ofs and so forth.
And when we got there, Dostert and I realized very quickly we were gonna have to send a lot of these people right straight back home.
They were useless.
And so I got this boy in there, Wallie Cohn, who was Jewish, and whose father had been in one of those [INAUDIBLE] towns.
I've forgotten which.
And had fled in good time, got his family out.
But Wallie didn't have a good education in Germany or in-- or in the United States.
He spoke English and German, but both of a very rudimentary kind, you see.
And he wouldn't do for an interpreter or a translator at all.
So I was going-- running them down.
And this is about four or five days after Wallie had appeared.
And I called him into my office, and I said, Wallie, I guess you know what this is all about.
I said, you just can't cut the mustard as an either interpreter or as a translator, and we've got to send you home.
Now, I had put all these people that were to be sent home in a special assembly point which they called Siberia, you see.
And he says, oh, don't send me to Siberia.
Don't send me to Siberia.
I said, well, why not?
He says, have you got a car?
And I said, yes.
He says, is it in good shape?
I said, no, it's in terrible shape.
He says, let me have it for three days, huh?
I said, well what am I gonna use to get back and forth to the-- house and here?
He says, you can take my Jeep.
He'd been in town four days.
His Jeep, see.
This is Wallie's.
So all right, go.
Take the car.
It came back repainted, new springs.
They'd redone the engine.
They had re-upholstered it with-- guess what-- Army blankets.
And [LAUGHS] in the center of the dashboard-- now, get this.
This is a typical Kraut touch.
There was a cut-glass vase with a rose in it.
Jeez.
I said.
Wallie, you are now special administrative assistant.
[LAUGHS] And we're going to take a break.
